raw_case:
Prenatal magnetic resonance imaging of umbilical cord ulcer in a fetus with congenital small bowel atresia: A case report
A 34-year-old woman (gravida 2, para 0) with suspected fetal small-bowel atresia on ultrasound underwent magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) at 33 weeks 3 days of gestation. In addition to small bowel atresia, a teardrop-shaped hematoma was observed adjacent to the umbilical cord associated with an umbilical cord ulcer (UCU). The fetus was delivered by induced labor on diagnosis of intrauterine fetal death. UCUs were macroscopically observed on the surface of the umbilical cord. Ultrasonography is helpful in the diagnosis of UCU. Moreover, the MRI findings on UCUs adjacent to the umbilical cord in the case of congenital small bowel atresia should be observed carefully, especially after 30 weeks of gestation. MRI findings may lead to the detection of UCU and improve fetal outcome. Therefore, we should be aware of its findings.

Introduction

Umbilical cord ulcer (UCU) is associated with congenital small intestinal atresia, which is one of the most severe conditions leading to perinatal fetal death. Ultrasonography (US) is helpful for the early diagnosis of UCU [1]. US findings reported include fetal blood circling from the umbilical cord into the amniotic fluid, heterogeneous high-echo mass debris in the amniotic fluid, and hematoma in the fetal stomach. Although many case reports on the US diagnosis of UCU are available, to our knowledge, no case reports on diagnosis using MRI are available. Herein, we report MRI findings of a fetus with a UCU associated with congenital small intestinal atresia.

Case report

The patient was a 34-year-old woman (gravida 2, para 0) with an unremarkable medical history. She conceived through in vitro fertilization. US examination at 28 weeks 5 days of gestation revealed fetal intestinal dilatation, which is suggestive of congenital small intestinal atresia. She was referred to our hospital for further evaluation at 31 weeks 2 days gestation. US demonstrated the dilatation of the stomach and jejunum with a maximum diameter of 18.6 mm. The amniotic fluid index (AFI) was 17.59, not indicative of polyhydramnios. No cord or placental abnormalities were noted in the US. There were no other prenatal complications related to the pregnancy.Fetal MRI was performed at 33 weeks 3 days of gestation for further evaluation. After the MRI examination, she was given a discharge without further medical examination, including US, as she showed no symptoms. At first, a resident radiologist interpreted the MRI. He found the dilatation from the esophagus to the jejunum (Fig. 1A), which is suggestive of congenital jejunal atresia.A board-certified radiologist interpreted the MRI examination about half a day later. In addition to the findings of jejunal atresia, he recognized a teardrop-shaped area with iso-signal intensity on the T2-weighted image (Fig. 1B, C) and slightly high signal intensity on the fat-suppressed T1-weighted image (Fig. 1D) adjacent to the umbilical cord, which is suggestive of hematoma. UCU is a known complication associated with congenital small intestinal atresia. Therefore, the hematoma was suspected to be associated with a UCU due to atresia. However, the patient returned to our hospital at that time because of decreased fetal movement. Emergency US showed no fetal heart rate, suggesting intrauterine fetal death (IUFD). The fetus was delivered by an induced labor 4 days after the death. A large amount of bloody amniotic fluid (1300 ml) was discharged during the delivery of the fetus. The fetus was pale, but no external anomalies were observed.Elasticity of Wharton's jelly in the umbilical cord was observed to be decreased. Additionally, the degeneration and exposure of umbilical vessels with hemorrhages were grossly observed, which indicates UCUs on the superficial layer of the umbilical cord (Fig. 2A, B). The bloody amniotic fluid and fetal pallor were considered due to the hemorrhage from the umbilical cord.

Discussion

UCU is one of the most severe disorders associated with congenital small bowel obstruction, which can lead to fetal perinatal death. Congenital intestinal atresia occurs in approximately 1 case per 2,000-5,000 births. Meanwhile, a UCU is complicated by an upper gastrointestinal obstruction in 6.5-13.6% [2,3]. The etiology of a UCU associated with congenital small bowel obstruction is not well understood. However, 3 factors (vascular reactivity, gastric reflex, and epithelial abnormality) have been proposed to cause a UCU [4]. Reflux vomiting due to small bowel obstruction causes the release of bile acids and pancreatic enzymes into the amniotic fluid, which destroys the Wharton's jelly of the umbilical cord, resulting in the exposition and disruption of the umbilical vessels [[5], [6]]. The prognosis of the fetus is then very poor. Aronowitz et al. reported an intrauterine fetal death rate, a neonatal death rate, and an overall survival rate of 32%, 32%, and 37%, respectively [7].Early diagnosis is desirable because a UCU has a severe outcome. Various methods for early detection have been reported, although currently there is no gold standard. Katsura et al. reported that US is more valuable than a cardiotocogram (CTG) at the onset of a UCU [8]. Following US findings have been reported: fetal blood swirling from the umbilical cord into the amniotic fluid, heterogeneous high-echo mass-like debris in the amniotic fluid, and fetal stomach reflecting blood clots. These ultrasound imaging findings suggest that hemorrhage from a UCU can lead to an early diagnosis [8].In this present case, MRI incidentally showed an abnormal signal intensity around the umbilical cord. The signal pattern, the teardrop shape, and the location adjacent to the umbilical cord indicate the bleeding from the cord. These MRI findings, except signal intensity, are consistent with US findings. In addition, UCU has been reported to be located near the fetal side of the umbilical cord in most reported cases. Therefore, careful ultrasound evaluation of the area is advisable in high-risk cases [[9], [10]]. Bleeding from the UCU near the fetal side was also seen on the MRI in our case. Abnormal signal intensities around the fetal side of the umbilical cord can help diagnose a UCU. However, the signal characteristics of acute hemorrhage can vary, leading to potential misinterpretation of signal intensity. Additionally, small hematomas can be challenging to detect. These limitations of MRI should be taken into account.MRI should not necessarily be recommended, as US is useful in the diagnosis of UCU. However, it may be performed for prenatal evaluation of congenital small bowel atresia. The UCU may have been incidentally detected on an MRI in this case. The mean gestational age at the presentation of UCU is 33.3 ± 2.7 weeks. Consequently, strict follow-up is required after 30 weeks of gestation [11]. US evaluations and the measurement of gastrointestinal enzyme levels are performed to determine delivery timing [12].Moreover, we should pay attention to the findings of UCU and recognize that it can occur even without the mother showing any symptoms. Thus, we should evaluate the bleeding from a UCU in the case of congenital small bowel obstruction after 30 weeks of gestation immediately after the MRI examination. If UCU can be detected on MRI and reported to the obstetrician, appropriate clinical management may improve the fetus's prognosis.In conclusion, we should take notice of the MRI findings associated with a UCU in the evaluation of a fetus with congenital small bowel atresia, especially after 30 weeks of gestation.

Patient consent

Informed consent was obtained from the patient.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 34-year-old woman, gravida 2, para 0. Conceived via in vitro fertilization.  
- **Chief Complaint:** Decreased fetal movement.  
- **History of Present Illness:** Suspected fetal small bowel atresia noted on ultrasound (US) at 28 weeks 5 days of gestation, confirmed by intestinal dilatation findings. Referred for further evaluation at 31 weeks 2 days. Fetal MRI at 33 weeks 3 days showed jejunal atresia and a teardrop-shaped hematoma adjacent to the umbilical cord, suggestive of umbilical cord ulcer (UCU).  
- **Past Medical History:** None reported.  
- **Personal History:** None reported.  
- **Family History:** None reported.  
- **Physical Examination:** Emergency ultrasound at return showed no fetal heart rate. Post-delivery examination revealed bloody amniotic fluid, pale fetus, and macroscopic changes in the umbilical cord consistent with UCU (e.g., decreased Wharton’s jelly, degenerated and hemorrhagic umbilical vessels).  
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - Ultrasound at 28 weeks 5 days: Showed intestinal dilatation, later interpreted as congenital small bowel atresia.  
  - Ultrasound at 31 weeks 2 days: Stomach and jejunal dilatation; maximum diameter size 18.6 mm.  
  - Fetal MRI at 33 weeks 3 days: Confirmed jejunal atresia and hematoma near the umbilical cord, likely associated with UCU.  
  - Postmortem findings: Large bloody amniotic fluid (1300 mL) and gross umbilical cord ulcerations.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Congenital Small Bowel Atresia (Jejunal Atresia):**  
   - Supported by findings of intestinal dilatation on both US and MRI. Typical presentation includes stomach and proximal bowel dilatation. Diagnosis was confirmed.  

2. **Umbilical Cord Ulcer (UCU):**  
   - Hematoma adjacent to the umbilical cord on MRI indicated potential UCU, a known complication of congenital small bowel atresia. Post-delivery findings of umbilical hemorrhage and cord vessel degeneration further confirmed this diagnosis.  

3. **Other Causes of Intrauterine Fetal Death (IUFD):**  
   - Placental insufficiency, infections, and genetic anomalies were less likely given the absence of associated findings on imaging or clinical history.  
   - UCU-associated hemorrhage was directly corroborated by postmortem findings, making it the primary cause of IUFD.  

4. **Polyhydramnios or Amniotic Abnormalities:**  
   - Initially ruled out by a normal amniotic fluid index (AFI) on US.

The differential diagnosis focused on congenital malformations and umbilical abnormalities, with imaging and clinical evidence guiding the identification of UCU as secondary to small bowel atresia.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The fetus was diagnosed with congenital jejunal atresia complicated by umbilical cord ulcer (UCU), leading to intrauterine fetal death. The hemorrhagic complications of the UCU and fetal exsanguination were the direct causes of death.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Congenital jejunal atresia with umbilical cord ulcer (UCU) leading to intrauterine fetal death (IUFD).

results.content:
### Reasoning:

Step 1: Understanding the Case
I have a 34-year-old woman who is gravida 2, para 0, meaning she's pregnant for the second time but has no previous live births. Her pregnancy was achieved through in vitro fertilization (IVF). The primary complaint is decreased fetal movement, which is a concerning symptom that can indicate fetal distress or other complications.

Step 2: Reviewing the History of Present Illness
The patient had an ultrasound at 28 weeks and 5 days that suggested fetal small bowel atresia due to intestinal dilatation. This was confirmed by another ultrasound at 31 weeks and 2 days, which showed stomach and jejunal dilatation with a maximum diameter of 18.6 mm. Fetal MRI at 33 weeks and 3 days confirmed jejunal atresia and also revealed a teardrop-shaped hematoma adjacent to the umbilical cord, indicating a possible umbilical cord ulcer (UCU).

Step 3: Considering the Past, Personal, and Family History
There are no reported past medical, personal, or family histories provided, which means I need to focus on the current pregnancy and its complications.

Step 4: Examining the Physical Examination Findings
During an emergency ultrasound, there was no fetal heart rate detected, indicating fetal demise. Post-delivery, the amniotic fluid was bloody, and the fetus appeared pale. The umbilical cord showed macroscopic changes consistent with UCU, including decreased Wharton's jelly and degenerated, hemorrhagic umbilical vessels.

Step 5: Reviewing Ancillary Tests
- Ultrasound at 28 weeks 5 days: Intestinal dilatation suggestive of small bowel atresia.
- Ultrasound at 31 weeks 2 days: Stomach and jejunal dilatation with a maximum diameter of 18.6 mm.
- Fetal MRI at 33 weeks 3 days: Confirmed jejunal atresia and a hematoma near the umbilical cord, suggestive of UCU.
- Postmortem findings: Large amount of bloody amniotic fluid (1300 mL) and gross umbilical cord ulcerations.

Step 6: Correlating Findings
The presence of fetal small bowel atresia and umbilical cord ulceration are both significant findings. Small bowel atresia is a congenital anomaly that can lead to bowel obstruction and other complications. Umbilical cord ulceration can compromise blood flow to the fetus, leading to fetal distress or death.

Step 7: Considering the Sequence of Events
The patient initially presented with decreased fetal movement, leading to the discovery of intestinal dilatation suggestive of small bowel atresia. Subsequent imaging confirmed this and also revealed a hematoma near the umbilical cord, suggesting UCU. Eventually, fetal demise was observed, with post-delivery examination confirming UCU.

Step 8: Differential Diagnosis
- Fetal small bowel atresia: Confirmed by ultrasound and MRI.
- Umbilical cord ulceration: Suggested by MRI and confirmed post-delivery.
- Fetal demise: Due to complications from either or both conditions.

Step 9: Determining the Cause of Fetal Demise
Given the findings, it's likely that the combination of fetal small bowel atresia and umbilical cord ulceration led to fetal distress and eventual demise. The UCU may have compromised blood flow, exacerbating the issues caused by the bowel atresia.

Step 10: Final Diagnosis
Based on the clinical presentation, imaging findings, and post-delivery examination, the final diagnosis is fetal small bowel atresia with associated umbilical cord ulceration leading to fetal demise.

### Answer:
Fetal small bowel atresia with umbilical cord ulceration leading to fetal demise.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: <Step 1> Intestinal dilatation observed on both US and MRI supported the diagnosis of congenital small bowel atresia (jejunal atresia), which was confirmed by findings of stomach and proximal bowel dilatation.

<Step 2> Hematoma adjacent to the umbilical cord on MRI indicated potential umbilical cord ulcer (UCU), a known complication of congenital small bowel atresia, and post-delivery findings of umbilical hemorrhage and cord vessel degeneration further confirmed this diagnosis.

<Step 3> Placental insufficiency, infections, and genetic anomalies were ruled out as causes of intrauterine fetal death (IUFD) due to the absence of associated findings on imaging or clinical history.
<Step 4> UCU-associated hemorrhage was directly corroborated by postmortem findings, establishing it as the primary cause of IUFD.
<Step 5> Polyhydramnios or amniotic abnormalities were ruled out based on a normal amniotic fluid index (AFI) observed on US.
<Step 6> The differential diagnosis focused on congenital malformations and umbilical abnormalities, with imaging and clinical evidence identifying UCU as secondary to small bowel atresia.
<Step 7> The fetus was diagnosed with congenital jejunal atresia complicated by umbilical cord ulcer (UCU), leading to intrauterine fetal death.
<Step 8> Hemorrhagic complications of the UCU and fetal exsanguination were determined to be the direct causes of death.
